title: Remote Distance Monitor
subtitle: Measure Distance Wirelessly and Watch It Live on Your Phone!
overview: What if you could monitor the distance to an object from anywhere in the world using your phone? In this session, students combine the HC-SR04 ultrasonic distance sensor with an ESP8266 and either a local web server or the Blynk IoT platform to build a real-time remote distance monitor. The live distance reading is streamed to a browser or smartphone dashboard — perfect as a proximity alarm, tank level monitor, or smart parking sensor.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — HC-SR04 wiring, Serial Monitor readings, browser dashboard, Blynk app screenshot, full circuit build.

keyTopics: HC-SR04 — An ultrasonic distance sensor that measures distance from 2 cm to 400 cm using sound pulses
TRIG Pin — The trigger pin: a 10-microsecond HIGH pulse on TRIG sends an ultrasonic burst of 8 pulses at 40 kHz
ECHO Pin — The echo pin: goes HIGH when the ultrasonic burst is sent and LOW when the reflection is received; the duration = time of flight
Time of Flight — The time taken for the ultrasonic pulse to travel to the object and return; used to calculate distance
Distance Formula — Distance (cm) = (pulse duration in microseconds) / 58.2 OR Duration × speed of sound / 2
pulseIn() — Arduino function that measures the duration (in microseconds) of a HIGH or LOW pulse on a pin; used to read ECHO
Speed of Sound — Approximately 340 m/s (or 0.034 cm/µs) at room temperature; the constant in the distance calculation
Voltage Divider — HC-SR04 ECHO pin outputs 5V but ESP8266 is 3.3V — a voltage divider (1kΩ and 2kΩ resistors) protects the ESP8266
Real-Time Streaming — Sending sensor data continuously to a browser or app so it updates live without manual refresh
Proximity Alert — Triggering an action (buzzer, LED, notification) when measured distance falls below a threshold
Blynk Gauge Widget — A circular meter widget in the Blynk app that displays a numeric value within a min/max range — ideal for distance
Use Cases — Tank level monitoring, smart parking sensors, blind-spot detection, intruder alert systems, conveyor belt object detection

resources.title: Random Nerd Tutorials — HC-SR04 with ESP8266

activity.title: Build a Remote Distance Monitor — Sensor to Web/App
parts: 1kΩ and 2kΩ resistors (for voltage divider on ECHO pin)
Active WiFi network with internet access
Smartphone with Blynk app (for extended challenge)
steps: Step 1 — HC-SR04 Wiring: Connect VCC → 5V (or Vin on NodeMCU), GND → GND, TRIG → D5 (GPIO14), ECHO → voltage divider middle point → D6 (GPIO12). Build voltage divider: ECHO pin → 1kΩ → D6, and 2kΩ from D6 to GND. This steps 5V down to ~3.3V

Step 2 — Distance Sketch: Write a basic sketch — define TRIG_PIN and ECHO_PIN. In loop(): send a 10µs HIGH pulse on TRIG, use pulseIn(ECHO_PIN, HIGH) to measure pulse duration, calculate distance = duration / 58.2. Print to Serial Monitor every 500ms
Step 3 — Serial Monitor Test: Upload and open Serial Monitor at 115200 baud. Move your hand closer and farther from the sensor — watch the distance values change live. Verify accuracy with a ruler
Step 4 — Add Proximity Alert: Add a buzzer on pin D7. If distance < 10 cm, activate buzzer and print 'ALERT: Object too close!'. If distance > 10 cm, buzzer off
Step 5 — Add WiFi: Include ESP8266WiFi.h, connect to WiFi, print the IP address. Create a WiFiServer on port 80

Step 6 — Web Dashboard: On each browser request, build an HTML response with the current distance value, a colour-coded bar (green > 50cm, yellow 20–50cm, red < 20cm), and a meta refresh every 2 seconds. Test in browser

Step 7 — Blynk Integration: Replace or add to the web server — use Blynk.virtualWrite(V0, distance) inside a BlynkTimer every 1 second. Add a Gauge widget in the Blynk app on V0, range 0–400 cm. Watch the gauge update live
Step 8 — Go Remote: Switch phone to mobile data and confirm the Blynk gauge still updates. You are now monitoring distance from anywhere!

Challenge: Add a Blynk notification — use Blynk.logEvent('proximity_alert') to send a push notification to your phone when distance drops below 10 cm. Also add a graph widget to visualise distance over time

quiz.q: What does the HC-SR04 sensor measure?
options: Temperature and humidity
Distance to an object using ultrasonic sound pulses
Light intensity
Magnetic field strength
quiz.explanation: HC-SR04 is an ultrasonic distance sensor! It emits a high-frequency sound pulse (40 kHz), waits for the echo to return from an object, and measures the time taken — converting it to a distance reading from 2 cm to 400 cm.

quiz.q: What is the function of the TRIG pin on the HC-SR04?
options: It receives the reflected ultrasonic pulse
It triggers the sensor to send an ultrasonic burst by receiving a 10-microsecond HIGH pulse
It outputs the calculated distance in centimetres
It provides power to the sensor
quiz.explanation: TRIG = trigger! Send a 10µs HIGH pulse to the TRIG pin and the sensor fires a burst of 8 ultrasonic pulses at 40 kHz. Think of it like pressing the shutter button on a camera — TRIG starts the measurement cycle.

quiz.q: What does the ECHO pin do?
options: It sends the ultrasonic pulse outward
It goes HIGH when the ultrasonic burst is sent and returns LOW when the reflection is received — the duration of this HIGH pulse represents the time of flight
It outputs a digital HIGH if an object is detected within range
It controls the frequency of the ultrasonic pulses
quiz.explanation: ECHO measures the round-trip time! It goes HIGH the moment the burst is sent and drops LOW when the echo returns. The duration of this HIGH pulse (measured in microseconds) is the time the sound took to travel to the object and back.

quiz.q: What Arduino function is used to measure the duration of the ECHO pin's HIGH pulse?
options: analogRead()
digitalRead()
pulseIn(ECHO_PIN, HIGH)
millis()
quiz.explanation: `pulseIn(pin, HIGH)` waits for the pin to go HIGH, starts timing, then stops when it goes LOW — returning the duration in microseconds. It's the perfect tool for reading the HC-SR04 ECHO pin's time-of-flight measurement.

quiz.q: Using the distance formula, if `pulseIn()` returns 1164 microseconds, what is the distance to the object?
options: 20 cm
582 cm
10 cm
quiz.explanation: Distance = duration / 58.2 → 1164 / 58.2 ≈ 20 cm! The formula divides by 58.2 because sound travels at ~0.034 cm/µs, and the pulse travels the distance TWICE (out and back). Dividing by 58.2 gives the one-way distance in centimetres.

quiz.q: Why must a voltage divider be used on the HC-SR04 ECHO pin when connecting to ESP8266?
options: To increase the signal strength for longer range
The HC-SR04 ECHO pin outputs 5V but the ESP8266 GPIO pins are only 3.3V tolerant — 5V would damage the chip
To slow down the signal so the ESP8266 can process it
The voltage divider is only needed for the TRIG pin, not ECHO
quiz.explanation: Critical safety step! The HC-SR04 runs on 5V and its ECHO pin outputs 5V logic. The ESP8266 GPIO pins are NOT 5V tolerant — connecting 5V directly can permanently damage the chip. A simple 1kΩ / 2kΩ voltage divider steps 5V down to ~3.3V safely.

quiz.q: What is Time of Flight in the context of the HC-SR04?
options: The time it takes to upload the sketch to the ESP8266
The total time for the ultrasonic pulse to travel from the sensor to the object and back again
The flight time of a drone using ultrasonic navigation
The time between two consecutive distance readings
quiz.explanation: Time of Flight = round-trip travel time of the sound pulse! Sound leaves the TRIG side, bounces off the object, and returns to the ECHO side. Dividing this time by 2 gives the one-way travel time. Multiply by the speed of sound (0.034 cm/µs) to get the distance.

quiz.q: What is the approximate range of the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor?
options: 0 to 10 cm
2 cm to 400 cm
1 m to 100 m
0.1 mm to 50 cm
quiz.explanation: HC-SR04 measures from 2 cm to 400 cm (4 metres)! Readings below 2 cm are unreliable because the sensor can't distinguish the outgoing pulse from the echo at very close range. Beyond 400 cm, the echo is too weak to detect reliably.

quiz.q: In the remote distance monitor project, how does `Blynk.virtualWrite(V0, distance)` work?
options: It writes the distance value to the physical pin V0 on the ESP8266
It sends the current distance value from the ESP8266 to the Blynk cloud, which updates any widgets linked to virtual pin V0 in the app
It saves the distance value to EEPROM on the ESP8266
It writes the distance to the Serial Monitor
quiz.explanation: `Blynk.virtualWrite()` pushes data FROM hardware TO the app! Calling `Blynk.virtualWrite(V0, distance)` inside a timer sends the latest distance reading to the Blynk cloud server, which instantly updates any Gauge, Value Display, or Graph widget linked to V0 in the app.

quiz.q: What is a Proximity Alert in this project?
options: A WiFi signal that warns when the ESP8266 is too far from the router
An action triggered when the measured distance falls below a set threshold — such as sounding a buzzer or sending a phone notification
An alert sent when the sensor reading exceeds 400 cm
A compiler warning about the pulseIn() function
quiz.explanation: Proximity Alert = distance threshold trigger! If `distance < 10` → buzzer on, LED flashes, or Blynk notification sent. This is exactly how real-world systems work: parking sensors beep faster as you get closer, security systems alert when someone enters a zone.

quiz.q: What real-world device uses the same ultrasonic time-of-flight principle as the HC-SR04?
options: A microwave oven
Parking sensors in cars that beep as you reverse toward an obstacle
A WiFi router measuring signal strength
A smoke detector
quiz.explanation: Car parking sensors! They use ultrasonic transducers on the bumper to measure distance to obstacles and beep faster as you get closer. Medical ultrasound, industrial level sensors, and some robot navigation systems all use the same time-of-flight sonic principle.

quiz.q: Why is it useful to add a meta refresh tag to the ESP8266 web dashboard for the distance monitor?
options: It resets the ESP8266 automatically if the distance reading fails
It makes the browser automatically reload the page every few seconds, displaying the latest distance reading without the user pressing refresh
It speeds up the WiFi connection between the browser and the ESP8266
It is required for the Blynk library to function correctly
quiz.explanation: Auto-refresh keeps the data live! Without it, the browser shows a static reading from when the page was last loaded. `<meta http-equiv='refresh' content='2'>` reloads every 2 seconds — giving a near-real-time display of the changing distance values.

quiz.q: A student's HC-SR04 consistently returns a distance of 0 cm or very large random numbers. What should they check first?
options: The WiFi SSID and password
The TRIG and ECHO pin numbers in the code match the actual wiring, the sensor has 5V power, and the ECHO voltage divider is correctly assembled
The Blynk auth token
The Serial Monitor baud rate
quiz.explanation: Bad readings = wiring first! Check: TRIG and ECHO pins in code match physical connections, sensor VCC is on 5V (not 3.3V — it needs 5V to work reliably), GND is connected, and the voltage divider is correctly assembled with 1kΩ and 2kΩ resistors in the right order.

quiz.q: What are three real-world applications that use remote distance monitoring on the same principle as this project?
options: Keyboard, mouse, and monitor
Water tank level monitoring, smart parking space availability sensors, and intruder detection alarm systems
WiFi router, Bluetooth speaker, and USB hub
Calculator, printer, and scanner
quiz.explanation: Remote distance monitoring has huge real-world value! A water tank sensor monitors fill level and alerts when low. Smart parking systems detect if a bay is occupied. Security systems trigger alerts when someone enters a restricted zone. Industrial conveyors detect object positions. All use the same time-of-flight principle!

quiz.q: What advantage does using Blynk for remote monitoring have over the local web server approach?
options: Blynk provides more accurate distance readings from the sensor
Blynk allows monitoring from anywhere with internet access, not just the same local WiFi network — and provides a purpose-built mobile dashboard with gauges and graphs
Blynk eliminates the need for a voltage divider on the ECHO pin
Blynk automatically calibrates the HC-SR04 sensor
quiz.explanation: Blynk = global access + rich dashboard! The local web server only works when phone and ESP8266 are on the same WiFi. With Blynk, your phone can be anywhere — distance data is routed through the cloud. Plus, Gauge and Graph widgets give a professional monitoring dashboard out of the box.
